coredata.dc:title: Electrochemiluminescence nanoemitters for immunoassay of protein biomarkers
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Chao
Liu, Songqin
Ju, Huangxian
coredata.dc:description: The family of electrochemiluminescent luminophores has witnessed quick development since the electrochemiluminescence (ECL) phenomenon of silicon nanoparticles was first reported in 2002. Moreover, these developed ECL nanoemitters have extensively been applied in sensitive detection of protein biomarker by combining with immunological recognition. This review firstly summarized the origin and development of various ECL nanoemitters including inorganic and organic nanomaterials, with an emphasis on metal-organic frameworks (MOFs)-based ECL nanoemitters. Several effective strategies to amplify the ECL response of nanoemitters and improve the sensitivity of immunosensing were discussed. The application of ECL nanoemitters in immunoassay of protein biomarkers for diagnosis of cancers and other diseases, especially lung cancer and heart diseases, was comprehensively presented. The recent development of ECL imaging with the nanoemitters as ECL tags for detection of multiplex protein biomarkers on single cell membrane also attracted attention. Finally, the future opportunities and challenges in the ECL biosensing field were highlighted.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The family of electrochemiluminescent luminophores has witnessed quick development since the electrochemiluminescence (ECL) phenomenon of silicon nanoparticles was first reported in 2002. Moreover, these developed ECL nanoemitters have extensively been applied in sensitive detection of protein biomarker by combining with immunological recognition. This review firstly summarized the origin and development of various ECL nanoemitters including inorganic and organic nanomaterials, with an emphasis on metal-organic frameworks (MOFs)-based ECL nanoemitters. Several effective strategies to amplify the ECL response of nanoemitters and improve the sensitivity of immunosensing were discussed. The application of ECL nanoemitters in immunoassay of protein biomarkers for diagnosis of cancers and other diseases, especially lung cancer and heart diseases, was comprehensively presented. The recent development of ECL imaging with the nanoemitters as ECL tags for detection of multiplex protein biomarkers on single cell membrane also attracted attention. Finally, the future opportunities and challenges in the ECL biosensing field were highlighted.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: As a kind of chemiluminescence phenomenon initiated by the electrochemical reaction taking place in the vicinity of electrode, electrochemiluminescence (ECL) inherits the high sensitivity from chemiluminescence method as well as the temporal and spatial controllability from electrochemistry, which also possesses the distinctive advantage of near-zero background signal compared with photoluminescence (PL) [1]. Since the first elaborate ECL study was published in the mid-1960s [2], extensive research efforts have been pour into the ECL field, and now ECL has grown into a rather powerful analytical technique, which has been universally applied in clinical diagnosis, food safety analysis, environmental monitoring [3]. The ECL mechanisms include two main classes, namely annihilation and coreactant pathways, among which the coreactant ECL with narrow potential window and strong ECL response brings the ECL phenomenon from fundamental research to practical applications. The ECL luminophores play a leading role in the ECL reaction process, which react with different coreactants or their intermediates to generate ECL emission. The development of nanomaterials has enriched the variety of ECL luminophores in last two decades. Initially, with the merits of high specific surface area and excellent conductivity, nanomaterials were employed as the carriers of Ru(bpy)3 2+ or luminol to amplify the ECL signal, and multitudinous nanocomposites of nanomaterials such as multiwall carbon nanotube [4], graphene oxide [5], Au nanoparticles (NPs) [6], incorporated with Ru(bpy)3 2+ or luminol, have been launched as ECL nanoemitters. Afterward, the pursuit of ECL luminophores with high ECL efficiency and low excitation potential has pushed the exploration of ECL nanoemitters from inorganic ECL nanoemitters (such as quantum dots, noble metal nanoclusters and carbon-based nanomaterials) to organic nanoemitters (such as polymer dots and metal organic frameworks).

A paramount breakthrough in the development of ECL is the ECL immunoassay which combines the specific immunoreaction with the intrinsic characters of ECL, enabling the sensitive and specific detection of biomarkers with fast analysis procedure and simple device. As a subcategory of biomarkers, protein biomarkers have become hot target analytes in ECL immunoassay. Various ECL biosensors, such as label-free ECL immunosensors, sandwich-type ECL immunosensors, competitive ECL immunosensors and even ECL aptasensors, have been developed for detection of protein biomarkers, including enzymes, hormones and other proteins. However, the contents of some biomarkers in the early stage of disease process are too low to be detected, and hence, effective signal amplification strategies are urgently needed to meet the demand for ultrasensitive quantification of biomarkers with extremely low level. The amplification for the ECL response of biosensors can be achieved by enhancing the ECL intensity of nanoemitters during the preparation of nanoemitters and the construction of ECL biosensors. More specifically, it can be achieved via preparing self-enhanced ECL nanoemitters to shorten the electron/energy transfer pathway, employing effective coreaction accelerators to hasten the decomposition of coreactants, or designing appropriate energy donor–acceptor pairs to realize resonance energy transfer. Moreover, the site-oriented immobilization of antibody has testified to be another effective method to enhance the ECL response of immunosensors for it can maintain the biological activities of antibodies and save incubation time during the immunoassay.

Different from the other recent reviews about the ECL of nanomaterials, in which the nanomaterials served as the carriers of emitters or the nanoemitters itself [7,8], and the reviews on ECL biosensors [9], this review mainly focuses on the development of ECL nanoemitters and their application in detection of protein biomarkers. The first part of this review summarizes the origin and development of various nanoemitters including inorganic and organic ECL nanoemitters, with an emphasis on metal–organic frameworks (MOFs)-based ECL nanoemitters. Then, several effective strategies to amplify the ECL intensity of nanoemitters and improve the sensitivity of immunosensing are discussed. Next, the applications of ECL nanoemitters in protein biomarker detection for diagnosis of cancers and other diseases, especially lung cancer and heart diseases, as well as the application of ECL nanoemitters in ECL imaging of protein biomarkers on single cell membrane are comprehensively presented. Finally, the future opportunities and challenges in the ECL biosensing field are highlighted for encouraging readers to exploit more excellent ECL nanoemitters and more sensitive biosensing devices. Particular interests are focused on the publications appeared from 2015 to 2022. We strongly recommend that readers refer to other excellent literatures to deepen the understanding on some specific themes [10–13].

In last two decades, the family of ECL nanoemitters have been expanded from classical ECL reagents such as Ru(bpy)3 2+ and luminol to nanoemitters including QDs, metal nanoclusters, MOFs. According to the composition of ECL nanoemitters, the ECL nanoemitters are mainly categorized into two types, inorganic ECL nanoemitters and organic ECL nanoemitters. Specifically, the inorganic ECL nanoemitters are classified into three groups, QDs, carbon-based nanomaterials and metal nanoclusters. As for organic ECL nanoemitters, they are divided into four groups, ECL micro/nanomaterials based on small organic molecules, MOFs-based ECL nanoemitters, covalent-organic frameworks (COFs)/hydrogen bond-organic frameworks (HOFs) based ECL nanoemitters, and polymer dots (Pdots), among which MOFs-based ECL nanoemitters are classified into three types, including MOFs with guests-induced ECL, MOFs with ligands-induced ECL, and MOFs with metal ions-induced ECL.

Since the ECL behavior of silicon quantum dots (QDs) in nonaqueous medium was reported firstly by Bard in 2002 [14], QDs have initiated great interest for their potential ECL application on account of their size-controlled photoluminescence and excellent stability against photobleaching. Soon after, Ju’s group proposed the first ECL biosensor for H2O2 detection by employing CdSe QDs as the emitter [15], which started a new era for fundamental research on ECL behavior of QDs and practical application of multifarious QDs in ECL biosensing. In 2008, Jie et al. [16] reported the first label-free CdSe QDs-based ECL immunosensor for human prealbumin (HPA) detection and broadened the way to employ QDs as emitters in ECL immunoassays. Later, a competitive immunosensor was constructed for human IgG (HIgG, which is commonly used in the therapy of immune and inflammatory diseases) detection by using CdTe QDs as cathodic emitters and H2O2 as the self-produced coreactant coupled with an enzymatic amplification strategy, which significantly improved the sensitivity of QDs-based ECL immunosensors [17]. Up to now, a series of III-V and II-VI QDs, such as CdSe [18–20], CdTe [21–25], PbS [26], CdS [27], ZnSe [28], CdSe@ZnS [29] and CdTe@ZnS [24] QDs, have been exploited as ECL nanoemitters. To satisfy the demand of biological applications, heavy-metal-free QDs, such as SnS2 [30,31], MoS2 [32], SnO2 [33], EuS [34], AgBr [35], black phosphorus [36] QDs, have been demonstrated to be ideal alternatives for developing ECL immunosensors. For example, Yuan et al. [31] designed an ECL immunosensor for cytomegalovirus pp65 antibody detection by self-assembly of SnS2 QDs on Ag nanoflowers and employing circular peptide-DNA nanomachine to amplify the ECL response, in which Ag nanoflowers acted as coreaction accelerators to boost the ECL signal of SnS2 QDs at low coreactant concentration (5 mM K2S2O8). Zou et al. [37] reported the coreactant-free ECL behavior of InP/ZnS QDs and used it for ECL immunoassay of prostate specific antigen (PSA, the most specific biomarker of prostate cancer), which opened up a promising ECL route beyond traditional annihilation and coreactant routes. In recent years, the list of QDs-based ECL emitters has been extended from the above binary QDs to ternary QDs (such as CuInS2/ZnS QDs [38]) and even to multinary QDs (such as MnZnAgInS/ZnS [39] and AgGaInS QDs [40]) with improved ECL performance, demonstrating a novel strategy to regulate the ECL behaviors of QDs.

With the merits of favorable biocompatibility and easy of functionalization, carbon-based nanomaterials have been considered as promising ECL candidates, which can be divided into three types, carbon dots (CDs), graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and graphene-like C3N4 nanosheets (g-C3N4 NSs). Since Chi et al. discovered the ECL phenomenon of CDs by a simple electrochemical method in 2009 [41], extensive efforts have been carried out to explore CDs-based ECL nanoemitters and amplify the ECL response of CDs. For example, by employing [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− as the electron/hole injector, an effective ECL signal amplification strategy for CDs was achieved in which [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− could facilitate the oxidation of CDs and convert S2O8 2− into SO4 •− expeditiously [42]. By using diethylene triamine pentacetate acid as the carbon and nitrogen source, N-doped CDs were prepared [43], in which nitrogen produced more surface traps on the surface of CDs to reduce NCDs more easily. With the improved ECL intensity and stability, NCDs served as cathodic ECL nanoemitters to fabricate a sensitive ECL immunosensor for HIgG detection. Although the ECL response of CDs was enhanced significantly by this method, it failed to lower the reduction potential of CDs. Contrarily, Chen et al. [44] synthesized nitrogen doped hydrazide-based CDs via a facile solvothermal method in which NCDs could emit strong anodic ECL at a lower excitation potential of +0.65 V, demonstrating a powerful method to improve the ECL efficiency of CDs. Inspired by this method, more heteroatom doped CDs have been developed as novel ECL nanoemitters, such as Ti, Mg@N-CDs [45], BCNO QDs [46] and N-Ti3C2 QDs [47]. In 2012, Zhu et al. [48] observed the ECL phenomenon of GQDs for the first time. Afterward, the anodic ECL behavior of hydrazide-modified GQDs in the presence of H2O2 as the coreactant [49] and the cathodic ECL behavior of GQDs in the presence of K2S2O8 as the coreactant [50] were also reported. Moreover, a dual potential ECL immunosensor was creatively fabricated by employing the strategy of electrochemical and chemical co-mediation for HIgG detection, based on the synchronous anodic and cathodic ECL behaviors of GQDs with two coreactants of S2O8 2− and SO3 2− [51]. Besides, Ding et al. [52] synthesized nitrogen- and sulfur- co-doped GQDs by a facile pyrolysis method in which the fluorescence quantum yield and ECL efficiency were 9.3-fold and 5.8-fold higher than the original GQDs, offering a new insight into improving the ECL performance of GQDs. In addition to CDs and GQDs, g-C3N4 NSs are also proved to be promising carbon-based ECL nanoemitters. Cheng et al. [53] first demonstrated the ECL behavior of g-C3N4 in 2013, and further modified the g-C3N4 NSs with Au NPs to construct a label-free ECL immunosensor for carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA, a broad-spectrum tumor biomarker) detection [54], in which Au NPs could catalyze the reduction of S2O8 2− and alleviate electron over-injection into g-C3N4. Later, a number of quench-type ECL immunosensors were developed for protein bioassay based on the resonance energy transfer from g-C3N4 to appropriate quenchers [55–57], revealing the great potential of g-C3N4 NSs in ECL immunosensing.

Compared with QDs and carbon-based nanomaterials, metal nanoclusters (NCs) are extremely attractive for biosensing due to their ultra-small size and nontoxicity. Robin et al. [58] pioneered the ECL study of Ag NCs in 2009, and meanwhile Xie et al. [59] proposed a facile preparation method for highly fluorescent Au NCs consist of 25 gold atoms (Au25 NCs) via using bovine serum albumin (BSA) to sequester and reduce Au precursors, which set the stage for exploration of noble metal NCs as ECL nanoemitters. In 2011, the ECL phenomenon of BSA-stabilized Au25 NCs were successively reported by Zhu et al. [60] and Chen et al. [61], and thus spurred extensive interest in this type of ECL nanoemitters. Ding’s group performed a series of in-depth and systematic researches on the near-infrared (NIR) ECL behaviors of Au NCs, such as Au18 [62], Au21 [63], Au25 [64], Au38 [65,66] NCs, which enriched the types of Au NCs and provided new insights into understanding the ECL mechanism of Au NCs. However, the low water solubility and limited ECL efficiency of Au NCs are also critical problems that hindered their further application in biosensing. To improve the ECL efficiency of Au NCs, Wang et al. [67] incorporated the coreactant into the lipoic acid-stabilized Au NCs by covalent coupling, which shortened the charge transfer distance and avoided excessive coreactant. More strikingly, with the strategy of pre-oxidation, the ECL efficiency of l -methionine (Met) stabilized Au NCs is much higher than most Au NCs emitters reported previously [68]. This strategy is applicable to other ligand-stabilized Au NCs, thus paving the way to enhance the ECL efficiency of Au NCs effectively. Meanwhile, Zou et al. [69] provided another method to accelerate the radiative charge transfer within Au NCs. That is, by employing 6-aza-2-thiothymine (ATT)-stabilized Au NCs as the host and l -arginine (ARG) as the guest, rigid host–guest assemblies were formed on the surface of Au NCs by hydrogen bonding, which restrained the non-radiative relaxation of excited Au NCs thus enhancing the ECL efficiency. Moreover, Liu et al. [70] found that by drying ATT-stabilized Au NCs on the electrode, the ECL efficiency of Au NCs could reach up to 78 % due to the enhanced electrochemical excitation by the electrocatalysis of triethylamine and solid state rigidification which reduced nonradiative relaxation. This dual-enhanced strategy is also suitable for other ligand-protected Au NCs, suggesting the enormous potential of Au NCs as ECL emitters in biosensing application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ECL micro/nanomaterials based on small organic molecules
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the low solubility and radical instability along with the narrow potential window of water, the ECL studies about small organic molecules-based micro/nanomaterials were not conducted until Bard proposed a reprecipitation method and an oil-in-water microemulsion method to synthesize aromatic hydrocarbon nanoparticles with ECL properties in aqueous solution in 2009 [90] and 2014 [91], respectively. Based on numerous ECL studies of organic molecules, the small organic molecules discussed in this review are classified into three groups, including polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs), tetraphenylethylene (TPE) and its derivatives, and heterocyclic aromatics. Pyrene, perylene (Pe), rubrene (Rub), 9,10-diphenylanthracene (DPA), and 3,4,9,10-perylenetetracarboxylic acid (PTCA, the derivative of Pe) are the most extensively studied PAHs with ECL properties. Drawing from Bard’s works mentioned above, various PAHs-based micro/nanomaterials were prepared as ECL emitters for biosensing, such as Rub microrods [92]/microblocks [93] /microsheets [94,95], Rub-decorated Ag@Au nanocubes [96], DPA-doped Pe microcrystals [97], perylene nanocrystals [98], DPA nanoblocks [99]/nanocubes [100] and PTCA nanorods [101]. For example, a highly sensitive ECL biosensor was constructed for quantification of digoxigenin antibody by employing Rub microblocks as the ECL probes and porous palladium nanospheres as the coreaction accelerators of dissolved O2 [93]. Noticeably, this work utilized a triplex-DNA nanomachine to convert the target antibody into cargo DNA as the mimic target. In this way, the cargo DNA could take part in the cycling strand displacement reaction for target amplification, thereby creating an effective pathway to improve the biosensing sensitivity. In another work, by assembling PTCA with aniline (An) via hydrogen bond, three ECL peaks were observed from the obtained ECL luminophore (PTCA-An), corresponding to the ECL emission from PTCA monomer, H-aggregates and J-aggregates, respectively [102]. Based on the effect of steric hindrance of protein on the peak intensities of ECL spectrum and the total ECL intensity, a label-free ECL immunosensor was established for CEA detection with a wide detection range from 1 pg·mL−1 to 10 μg·mL−1. Apart from PAHs-based luminophores, TPE-based micro [103]/nanocrystals [104,105] hold enormous promise in ECL sensing due to their intrinsic AIE characters. For example, the hexagonal TPE microcrystals exhibited a brighter ECL emission than the free TPE molecules owing to the enhancement of electron-hole recombination efficiency and the inhibition of nonradiative decay, and based on this principle, a signal “off–on” ECL sensor was designed for MUC1 detection by employing target-activated DNA walkers as the signal converters [103]. Nevertheless, it is difficult to active the inner ECL luminophores in the above bulky micro/nanomaterials during the electrochemical excitation, thus the ECL efficiency is still limited. To address this issue, various organic dots based on heterocyclic aromatics were explored as novel organic luminophores. Taking coumarin (a well-known electron acceptor) as an example, a coumarin derivative-based organic nanoparticle with a donor–acceptor structure was prepared as the ECL probe in which AIE-ECL emission was observed on account of its small size [106]. Moreover, taking the advantages of porous structure and good hydrophilicity, mesostructured silica xerogels (MSX) were chosen as the carriers to encapsulate abundant 1-pyrenecarboxaldehyde dots (PycDs) for ECL signal amplification [107]. The hydrophobic PycDs were enriched in the porous channels of MSX and reacted with coreactant intermediates more easily, thus an extremely strong ECL response was achieved.

With the merits of large specific surface area, well-ordered crystalline structure and accessible post-modification sites, MOFs have ignited an ever-increasing interest in ECL field. According to the ECL origin of MOFs, MOFs-based ECL nanoemitters can be divided into three groups, including MOFs with guests-induced ECL, MOFs with ligands-induced ECL, and MOFs with metal ions-induced ECL.

MOFs have been gradually emerged as promising carriers to load ECL emitters because their open channels make the diffusion of coreactant and its intermediates easier, and their porous structures endow all the active sites accessible. To date, a variety of MOFs have been chosen as the scaffolds to encapsulate Ru(bpy)3 2+, such as UiO-66 [108], UiO-66-NH2 [109], UiO-67 [110], MOF-5 [111], ZIF-8 [112], MIL-101 [113] and PCN-777 [114]. These carriers of Ru(bpy)3 2+ can also be classified into Zn- [111,112,115,116], Zr- [108–110,114,117], Al- [113], Hf- [118] based MOFs, among which Zr-based MOFs are the most popular ones for their desirable stability, and 2D nanosheets of Hf-MOFs have been developed with shorter electron transport distance and higher luminophores utilization ratio compared with bulky 3D MOFs [118]. In addition to Ru(bpy)3 2+, other prevalent ECL reagents, luminol and its derivative, can also been incorporated into MOFs. The resulted Fe-/Co-based MOFs not only act as the carriers of luminophores, but also serve as the coreaction accelerators to speed up the decomposition of coreactants [119–122]. Apart from the aforementioned ECL reagents, different ECL nanoemitters have also been loaded into MOFs, such as CdTe QDs [123], Au NCs [124], Pdots [125], PTCA nanorods [126], carbon nitride nanosheets [127] and porphyrin [128]. The in-depth study on how to improve the stability and electrochemical activity of MOFs needs to be further explored for the development of MOFs as ideal ECL emitters.

Ru(bpy)3 2+ can act as not only the guest to be encapsulated in MOFs, but also the ligand to prepare MOFs with ligands-induced ECL properties, which was proved by Xiong et al. in 2015 [129]. In addition to Ru(bpy)3 2+, the aforementioned small organic molecules that are used to prepare ECL nanocrystals can also serve as the ligands of MOFs to realize ligands-induced ECL. Except for rubrene, the derivatives of pyrene (Fig. 2 A) [130], perylene (Fig. 2B) [131] and DPA (Fig. 2C) [132,133] have been employed as the luminescent ligands to synthesize MOFs with improved ECL responses compared with the single small organic molecules. Impressively, Zhu et al. [133] employed two ligands, DPA as the emitter and 1,4-diazabicyclo[2.2.2]octane (dabco) as the coreactant, to design mixed-ligand MOFs with self-enhanced ECL performance in which an 26.5-fold ECL enhancement was observed owing to the intra-reticular charge transition among the two ligands (Fig. 2D).

The derivatives of TPE, such as 1,1,2,2-tetra(4-carboxylbiphenyl)ethylene (H4TCBPE) (Fig. 3 A and B) [134,135], 4′,4″,4‴,4⁗-(ethene-1,1,2,2-tetrayl)tetrakis(([1,1′-biphenyl]-4-carboxylic acid)) (H4ETTC) (Fig. 3C) [136], and 1,1,2,2-tetrakis(4-(pyridin-4-yl)phenyl)-ethene (TPPE) (Fig. 3D) [137] are also applied as the luminescent ligands of MOFs for ECL biosensing. The coordination of luminescent ligands in the rigid MOF matrix can restrict the intramolecular motions of ligands, restrain the nonraditive decay, and increase the loading capacity of ECL emitters. Hence, MOFs with ligands-induced ECL can be considered as ideal ECL nanoemitters for immunosensing. In particular, as a kind of macrocyclic compounds, porphyrins and its derivative, tetrakis (4-carboxyphenyl) porphyrin (TCPP), are also promising ligands to synthesize luminescent MOFs due to their unique catalytic, electrochemical and optical properties [138]. For example, by using pure TCPP as the linker and polyamidoamine dendrimer as the coreaction accelerator, a TCPP-based Ce-MOF was designed as the ECL emitter for sensitive detection of proprotein convertase subtilisin/kexin type 9 (PCSK9), the biomarkers of cardiovascular diseases (Fig. 3E) [139]. To be noted, considering that TCPPs with electrocatalytic activities can produce singlet oxygen (1O2) during cathodic ECL process [140,141], when discussing the cathodic ECL behaviors of TCPP-based MOFs, researchers need to figure out whether the ECL emitter is the excited (1O2)* or TCPP* [142–144].

The MOFs with metal ions-induced ECL are mainly referred to as lanthanide MOFs (Ln-MOFs), which possess unique PL properties such as relatively long luminescent time and high PL efficiency. Bard et al. [145] conducted the first ECL studies on the luminescent lanthanide complexes in 1996, in which the excited states of lanthanide complexes were formed by the electrochemical formation of ligands excited states and then the ligands sensitized the metal center via energy transfer or so-called “antenna effect”. This discovery laid the foundation for the research on ECL behavior of lanthanide nanomaterials and provided some inspiring ideas for the development of lanthanide-based ECL emitters. Hitherto, among the fifteen lanthanide elements, only the ECL behaviors of Eu- [146–150], Tb- [151–155], Yb- [156] and La- [157] based MOFs have been reported. Due to the low absorption coefficients of lanthanide ions, the ligand, as the “antenna” to transfer the excitation energy, plays an essential role in the ECL performance of lanthanide MOFs. For example, by using 4′-(4-carboxyphenyl)-2,2′:6′,2″-terpyridine as the ligand and Tb3+ as the metal ion, Li et al. [151] proposed a convenient strategy to prepare Tb-based metal organic gel and observed its strong green ECL emission in the presence of K2S2O8 as the coreactant. A Cu2+ doped Tb-MOF (Cu:Tb-MOF) with porous and hollow structure was prepared by employing isophthalic acid as the ligand, in which the Cu2+/Cu+ couple could accelerate the decomposition of K2S2O8 [155]. Meanwhile, Wei’s group [146–149] explored a large family of Eu-based MOFs with ECL properties in which the Eu3+ luminescence could be sensitized effectively by different ligands. Considering the abundant lanthanide elements in nature, the in-depth studies on the ECL behaviors of lanthanide-based MOFs need further exploration.

As a burgeoning class of organic porous crystal materials, COFs have drawn widespread interest over the last few years on account of their low density, adjustable porosity and metal-free characters. In 2020, Li et al. [158] proposed the first COFs-based ECL sensor by introducing AIE groups into the COFs, and the resulted COFs could emit strong anodic ECL with H2O2 as the coreactant and Co3O4 nanozyme as the coreaction accelerator. Similarly, Zhang et al. [159] synthesized a COF-based nanosheet (CON) by connecting pyrene (ACQ luminophore) with phenylenevinylene derivative (AIE luminophore) topologically, which exhibited a 2.8-fold stronger ECL intensity compared with the corresponding COF. In 2021, Qiu et al. [160] and Lei et al. [161] successively designed COFs-based ECL emitters with electron donor–acceptor structures by using non-ECL active monomers, which created a wealth of possibilities for COFs available in ECL sensing. Later, Qiu et al. [162,163] observed the ECL phenomenon of COFs with different donor–acceptor building blocks in the presence of dissolved O2 as the coreactant. To improve the poor conductivity of COFs, Xiao et al. [164] developed a kind of pyrazine-linked COFs with fully π-conjugated structure, which displayed high conductivity and bright cathodic emission after pre-reduction electrolysis, for constructing an ultrasensitive ECL sensing method for thrombin quantification. In the same way as MOFs and COFs, HOFs, which are prepared by organic ligands self-assembly through hydrogen-bonding interactions, have been recognized as promising candidates to MOFs- or COFs-based ECL emitters. Though HOFs have been applied in small-molecule sensing and gas storage extensively, research on HOFs-based ECL emitters is still in the initial stage. Feng et al. [165] provided the only one example about using triazinyl-based HOFs as the ECL luminophores, which inspires us to explore more HOFs-based ECL emitters.

Since Bard et al. [166] reported the first ECL studies of poly(9,9-dioctylfluorene-co-benzothiadiazole) (F8BT/PFBT) Pdots in 2008, Pdots with bright luminescence, desirable biocompatibility and superb photostability, have mushroomed over the last decade as a novel class of ECL nanoemitters. Chi et al. [167] pioneered the first ECL application of hydrophilic Pdots in aqueous solution by capping poly[2-methoxy-5-(2-ethylhexyloxy)-1,4-phenylenevinylene] (MEH-PPV) with triton X-100 through a reprecipitation method. To boost the ECL efficiency of MEH-PPV Pdots, Feng et al. [168] modified the MEH-PPV polymer with an electron-withdrawing cyano (CN) group, thus the resulted CN-PPV Pdots exhibited extraordinary annihilation and coreaction ECL emission. Meanwhile, the silole based Pdots with relatively low oxidation potential (Fig. 4 A) [169], and the Ru(bpy)3 2+ doped poly(9,9-dioctylfluorenyl-2,7-diyl) (PFO) Pdots with energy resonance transfer mechanism (Fig. 4B) [170], were also proposed by him to broaden the application of Pdots in ECL biosensing. Later on, based on the commercial-available polyfluorene polymers, various Pdots have been explored as ECL nanoemitters for biosensing, such as PFBT Pdots (Fig. 4C) [171–173] and PFO Pdots [174–177]. Moreover, designing the conjugated polymer backbones with an AIE-active moieties [178,179] or with an electron/energy donor–acceptor structure [125,180,181] were also proved to be effective strategies to boost the ECL efficiency of Pdots. Considering that the coreactant also plays a vital function in the ECL process, several innovative coreactant-free systems were devised to improve the ECL response of Pdots without adding exogenous coreactants. For instance, by adding two tertiary amine (TEA) groups into the side chain of PFBT polymer unit, coreactant-embedded TEA-PFBT Pdots (Fig. 4D) with a dual intramolecular electron transition mechanism was designed by our group for the first time to achieve the in situ ECL imaging of human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 (HER2, an important biomarker of breast cancer) membrane protein on individual living cell [182]. Compared with the intermolecular electron transition between exogenous coreactants and Pdots, the TEA-Pdots showed a 132-fold ECL intensity owing to the shortened electron transition distance and faster electron transition rate, which provides inspirations for the development of Pdots as ECL nanoemitters. Very recently, benefiting from the inner hydrophobic cavity of β-cyclodextrin (β-CD), Chen et al.[183] proposed another strategy to embed coreactants by hydrophobic localized enrichment and covalent linkage of coreactants in the cavities of β-CD for preparation of sensitive ECL immunosensor for SARS-CoV-2 nucleocapsid protein (ncovNP) using poly (2,5-dioctyl-1,4-phenylene)-based Pdots as emitter (Fig. 4E). However, the Pdots mentioned above mostly employed poly(styrene-co-maleicanhydride) (PSMA) as the copolymer to provide carboxyl on the surface of Pdots via weak noncovalent interaction, which might cause the falloff of emitters from the inner core. In this regard, carboxyl functionalized Pdots (Fig. 4F) [178] were prepared by linking carboxyl groups on the side chain of polymer to provide better compactness of ECL emitters insides the Pdots [184,185], which simplified the preparation process of Pdots and enriched the species of Pdots-based ECL emitters.

The emergence of ECL nanoemitters has catalyzed the vigorous development of ECL bioassays. However, in some cases, the ECL intensity of some nanoemitters is too weak to be applied in ECL bioassays. Thus, effective signal amplification strategies are urgently needed to meet the demand for ultrasensitive quantification of biomarkers with extremely low level. This part provides several effective strategies for signal amplification in ECL bioassays.

The most frequently-used coreactants in ECL systems can be categorized into two groups, “reductive-oxidation” coreactants such as peroxydisulfate (S2O8 2−), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and dissolved O2, and “oxidative-reduction” coreactants such as tripropylamine (TPrA) and sulfite (SO3 2−). In order to enhance the reaction efficiency between ECL emitters and coreactants, Yuan et al. [186] put forward a concept named “coreaction accelerator” in 2015, which referred to a chemical species that reacted with the coreactants, rather than the luminophores, to facilitate the decomposition of coreactants for more active intermediates generation. Up to now, various coreaction accelerators have been applied to facilitate the reduction of S2O8 2− for abundant oxidative intermediates (SO4 ·−) generation, such as Ag-based nanomaterials including Ag+ [187], Ag nanoparticles [107], and Ag nanoflowers [31], Cu-based nanomaterials including Cu2O [76], Pd@Cu2O [79], CuS [188], and MoS2@Cu2S [39], Fe-based nanomaterials including CoFe2O4 [189], hemin [101] and Fe3O4@CeO2 [84], other metallic oxides including TiO2 [190] and MnO2 [191], amine compounds such as 2-amino terephthalic acid in IRMOFs [123], NH2-MIL(Fe) MOFs [126], aniline [192], semicarbazide [186] and Bu4NPF6 [159], among which the amino compounds can be consumed in the reduction reaction process due to their irreversible electrochemical properties, while the metal complexes with excellent reduction reversibility can trigger an avalanche-like reaction to produce massive SO4 ·− for their reversible redox couples. For example, semicarbazide with two –NH2 terminal groups has been employed as the coreaction accelerator of CdTe QDs/S2O8 2− system, in which semicarbazide increases the reduction current of S2O8 2− significantly and enhances the ECL response of CdTe QDs by 1.46 times [186]. In another work, the synergic catalysis of MoS2 and Cu2S towards S2O8 2− results in the enhancement of ECL intensity of MnZnAgInS/ZnS QDs by 1.8 times [39]. However, in these ternary ECL systems, the coreaction accelerators and the luminophores are mostly not in the same nanostructures, thus the long distance between ECL emitters and active intermediates may cause energy wastage. In this respect, the element doping in the preparation of ECL emitters can be an effective strategy to address this issue, in which the resulted nanomaterials can act as both the emitters and the coreaction accelerators. For instance, Sun et al. [46] prepared BCNO QDs as the emitters, in which the boron radicals (B•) could catalyze the decomposition of S2O8 2− (Fig. 5 A). With the increase of C/B molar ratio from 0.25 to 1.00, the ECL efficiency of BCNO QDs in the prescence of 0.5 mM K2S2O8 was improved from 0.53 % to 5.57 %. Xue et al. [193] proposed an europium doped GdPO4 as the luminophores with low-potential ECL emission, in which the Eu3+/Eu2+ promoted the reduction of S2O8 2−, thus enhanced the ECL intensity of GdPO4 by 3-time (Fig. 5B). Wang et al. [155] prepared a hollow and porous Cu:Tb-MOF by doping Cu2+ in the Tb-based MOF, in which Cu2+ not only facilitated the reaction between Tb-MOF and S2O8 2− but also regulated the morphology of MOF facilely (Fig. 5C).

In addition to the coreaction accelerators for K2S2O8, numerous coreaction accelerators have been launched for H2O2 (the exclusive coreactant of luminol and its derivatives), such as ZnO nanorods [194], Ce:ZnO nanoflowers [195], CuS porous nanosheres [196], Co-based MOF [121] and Fe-based nanomaterials [197–199]. For instance, under the same conditions, the ECL response of CuS/ABEI/H2O2 ternary system was 2.05 times higher than that of the ABEI/H2O2 binary system. As an endogenic coreactant, dissolved O2 exhibits unique advantages of mild reaction environment and facile operation procedure. However, considering the slow conversion efficiency of dissloved O2 into O2 •− and relatively lower reaction activity of dissolved O2 than that of H2O2 and K2S2O8, the coreaction accelerators can play pivotal roles in enhancing the ECL response of emitter/dissolved O2 system. For instance, Yuan’s group [92,93,96] employed different noble-metal based nanomaterials as the coreaction accelerators to improve the ECL intensities of rubrene/dissolved O2 systems. Yang et al. [200,201] took the advantages of heterostructures of CeO2/SnS2 and oxygen defects of CoFe2O4 to promote the ECL responses of luminol/dissolved O2 systems, in which CeO2/SnS2 heterostructures led to a 2-time stronger ECL intensity than that of luminol/dissolved O2 systems, while CoFe2O4 with higher density of oxygen defects achieved 6.5-time stronger ECL response than the CoFe2O4 without oxygen defects. Other metallic oxides, such as TiO2 nanoflowers [202], MnOx nanoflowers [203] and Ag3BiO3 nanocrystals [204], have also been utilized as mimic peroxidases to hasten the decomposition of dissolved O2. Compared with various coreaction accelerators for S2O8 2−, H2O2 and dissolved O2, the development of coreaction accelerators for TPrA is still in its infancy. For example, Pd@CuO [83] and Fe2O3 nanoarrays [74] have been introduced into the Au NCs/TAEA system respectively to catalyze the oxidization of TAEA. By encapsulating hydrophobic iridium complexes into the core of apoferritin, Yang et al. [205] successfully prepared an Ir-based nanodot and observed its ECL behavior with TPrA as coreactant. Moreover, with Ti3C2Tx MXenes/TiO2 nanosheet as the coreaction accelerator, 2-time stronger ECL response was achieved due to its electroactive and catalytic properties.

Regardless of the great breakthrough of coreaction accelerators made in amplifying the ECL intensity of luminophores, some important problems should be considered seriously for further development. Firstly, with the rapid development of nanomaterials, extensive efforts should be paid on investigating the relationship between the size, morphology, composition of nanomaterials-based coreaction accelerators and the ECL intensity of emitters. Secondly, by using various characterizations, such as electron paramagnetic resonance measurement, electrochemical experiment and different radical scavengers, an in-depth and systematic study on the generation and evolution process of the coreactant active intermediates produced with the help of coreaction accelerators should be carried out to interpret the ECL signal amplification mechanism.

The traditional ECL system is based on the intermolecular electron transfer reaction between the ECL emitters and the coreactants by mixing the coreactants with the electrolyte directly, in which the long electron transfer pathway may cause energy loss, reagent waste and even measurement error. This situation can be skillfully solved by developing self-enhanced ECL emitters, in which the emitter and the coreactant are conjugated in one nanostructure to shorten the electron transfer distance, minimize the energy loss and simplify the detection procedure. The first report about self-enhanced ECL emitter could be traced back to 1996, when Liang et al. [206] discovered that ampicillin could act as the coreactant of Ru(bpy)3 2+. In 2012, Ding et al. [207] employed two dimethylamino groups in a iridium complex as the coreactants to exploit another self-enhanced ECL emitter, which exhibited three ECL emission peaks in its spooling ECL spectra. Later, different alkyl amines, such as polyethylenimine (PEI) (Fig. 6 A) [208–211], polyamidoamine (PAMAM) (Fig. 6B) [212], tris(3-aminopropyl)amine (TAPA) (Fig. 6C) [213], N,N-diisopropylethylenediamine (DPEA) (Fig. 6D) [214] and TAEA (Fig. 6E) [215], were linked with Ru(bpy)3 2+ to synthesize self-enhanced ECL emitters for ECL biosensor fabrication. Among them, PEI is the most popular linker due to its amine-rich and anionic polyelectrolyte characters, and various nanocomposites based on Ru(bpy)3 2+/PEI system have been developed for ECL biosensing, such as Ru-PEI@ZIF-8 [112], MIL-101(Al):Ru-PEI-Au [113], PEI@Ru-PCN-777 [114], Ru-based hollow nanospheres [216], Ru-Si nanoparticles (Fig. 6F) [217] and TiO2@PEI-Ru [218]. Meanwhile, PEI has proved to be the coreactant of N-(aminobutyl)-N-(ethylisoluminol) (ABEI) (Fig. 6G) [219], and Ru-PEI-ABEI complexes (Fig. 6H) have been proposed with excellent ECL efficiencies due to the intramolecular coreaction between Ru(bpy)3 2+ and PEI as well as the intramolecular ECL resonance energy transfer from ABEI to PEI [220]. Apart from alkyl amines, amino acid was also proven to be a possible coreactant of both Ru(bpy)3 2+ and ABEI, and several self-enhanced ECL systems based on this have been devised, such as ABEI- l -cysteine complex (Fig. 6I) [221] and Ru-Lys-Zr-MOL [117]. Moreover, N-doped CDs with abundant amine groups were also proposed to function as both the carrier and the coreactant of Ru(bpy)3 2+ [222]. Yang et al. [223] proposed a self-enhanced ECL system with dual coreactants, in which the branched PEI coated CDs were connected with Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ covalently (Fig. 6J) to boost the ECL response of Ru(bpy)3 2+ by 2.7-time. In addition to luminol and Ru(bpy)3 2+, other ECL emitters could also be used to develop self-enhanced ECL systems. For instance, the conjugation of N,N-diethylethyllenediamine on lipoic acid stabilized Au NCs (Fig. 6K) resulted in a 1.4-fold higher ECL efficiency than 6 μM Ru(bpy)3 2+ with 1 mM TPrA as the coreactant [67]. Similarly, an Au NCs-TAEA-Pd@CuO ternary ECL nanocomposite was proposed by employing TAEA as the bridge to linkage Au NCs and Pd@CuO nanomaterials, and a 3.4-fold ECL enhancement was achieved owing to the intramolecular coreaction among Au NCs and TAEA [83]. Moreover, the self-enhanced tertiary amine conjugated PFBT Pdots [182] and mixed-ligand MOFs with DPA as the luminophore and dabco as the coreatant [133] were proposed recently, which provided a steady stream of inspiration for creating more self-enhanced ECL nanoemitters.
(Here Fig. 6).

The occurrence of resonance energy transfer (RET) in ECL systems relies on a considerable spectral overlap between the ECL emission spectrum of energy donor and the UV–vis absorption spectrum of energy acceptor as well as a suitable distance between energy donor and acceptor (normally less than 10 nm). Since Bard et al. [224] reported the first ECL energy transfer phenomenon from a heteroexcimer to an Eu-based chelate in 1975, numerous energy donor–acceptor pairs have been exploited for ECL biosensing. For instance, Au-based nanomaterials have been widely functioned as the energy acceptors of QDs due to the wide absorption range induced by localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) [225–227]. By employing CdTe/CdS QDs with the ECL emission peak at 707 nm as the energy donors, and Au nanorods with SPR absorption bands at 661/700/763 nm as the acceptors, Wang et al. [228] systematically studied the effects of spectral overlapping area and the distance between donor and acceptor on the ECL quenching efficiencies, and fabricated an ultrasensitive aptasensor for thrombin determination, which showed a detection limit of 31 aM. QDs can also act as the energy acceptors owing to their wide absorption range and adjustable ECL emission peak. By using ABEI as the donor and QD as the acceptor, a ECL-RET method was built to investigate the interactions among proteins and the configuration changes of proteins [229]. Moreover, Ru(bpy)3 2+-based nanomaterials, especially the RuSi NPs, have also been extensively employed as the energy donor in the ECL-RET systems [230–233]. In the initial studies, the energy donor–acceptor pairs were ususlly in independent nanostructures, which might limit the RET efficiency and increase the energy loss. To solve this issue, several nanomaterials, in which the RET process took place in the same nanostructure or molecule, were proposed. For instance, to shorten the distance among donor–acceptor pairs, Chen et al. [234] assembled the energy donor (Ru(bpy)3 2+) and the acceptor (CdTe QD) in the same Si NPs, which improved the ECL intensity of Ru@SiO2 by nearly 12-fold. Similarly, Yuan’s group developed a series of ECL-RET models, such as Ru-PEI-ABEI [220], luminol-Ru [235], CdSe@ZnS QDs-Ru [29] nanostructures, to boost the energy transfer efficiencies. Notably, our group designed a number of multi-conponent luminescent polymers with donor–acceptor structures and further employed them to prepare Pdots-based ECL emitters with high ECL efficiencies [125,170,180]. For example, the triple-component Pdots with two pairs of energy donors and acceptors exhibited 380 or 31 times higher ECL intensity than that of no or single RET system [125]. In addition, a considerable effort has been devoted to explore novel nanomaterials as energy donors or acceptors, such as g-C3N4 NSs [236], Zr-TCBPE-MOF [237] and N-doped GQDs [238].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Site-oriented immobilization of antibodies for improving bioassay sensitivity
ce:section.ce:para: The immunoassays of protein biomarkers usually involve in the immunological recognition reaction between the immobilization antibody (Ab1) and antigen for label-free immunosensor or between the immobilization antibody (Ab1)-antigen-capture antibody (Ab2) for “sandwich-like” immunosensor. The immunological recognition capabilities of immunosensor towards target protein are dependent on the amounts of antibodies, and moreover, the biological activities of antibodies. The Fab fraction of antibody is the most active part which could specifically interact with the antigenic determinant. However, the random immobilization of antibodies in traditional immunosensor might result in the partial or full participation of the Fab fraction in the immobilization process, thus destroying the biological activity of antibodies. Bearing these facts in mind, in 2019, Wei’s group [75,199,200,239–241] proposed a site-oriented immobilization strategy for antibodies to improve the availability of antibodies in immunoreaction by using HWRGWVC hexa-peptides (HWR) as an effective antibody capturer. For instance, with the assistance of HWR peptides, an ultrasensitive ECL immunosensor was built for procalcitonin (PCT, a biomarker for diagnosis and monitoring of bacterial inflammatory disease infection) determination with detection limit down to 41 fg·mL−1 (Fig. 7 ) [199]. Specifically, the C terminal of HWR peptide with a cysteine could link with Au-based nanomaterials through Au-S bonds, while another end of this peptide could identify the amino acids in the Fc fraction of antibodies with strong affinity and leave the Fab fraction of antibodies undisturbed. In this way, the biological activities of antibodies were well-maintained, meanwhile, the dosage of antibodies used in the immobilization process was saved and the incubation time of antigen–antibody immunoreaction was shortened by 10 times, which were verified by HPLC and circular dichroism spectra experiments. Apart from HWR peptides, protein ligands such as protein A were also proved to be excellent antibody capturers [76], offering valuable opportunities to immobilize various antibodies targeting the corresponding antigens efficiently.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of ECL nanoemitters in detection of protein biomarkers
ce:section.ce:para: Protein biomarkers are a kind of substances that are secreted in the process of disease occurrence and even deterioration, or abnormal changes of the body in response to diseases, which reflect the existence and process of diseases, and are recognized as reliable dynamic indexes to diagnose diseases. In some points, protein biomarkers can be classified into three types, enzymes, hormones and other proteins. Here, we summarized the application of ECL nanoemitters in detection of disease biomarkers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of ECL nanoemitters in tumor biomarker detection
ce:section.ce:para: The most extensively studied protein-related tumor biomarkers in ECL bioassays include CEA, carbohydrate antigen (CA), alpha fetoprotein, prostate-specific antigen (PSA) and mucin 1 (MUC1). CEA is a glycoprotein with the molecular mass of 200 kDa, and its level in the patients’ blood who suffer from pancreatic, colorectal or breast cancer is abnormally higher than that in healthy individuals. Nowadays, great efforts have been devoted to developing highly-sensitive ECL immunosensors for CEA detection [23,83,136,212,242]. For instance, our group established a coreactant-dependent ratiometric ECL immunosensor by using CdS QD as cathodic luminophore and luminol as anodic luminophore to realize the detection of CEA with a detection limit of 0.62 pg·mL−1 [243]. Zhang et al. [19] used CdSe QDs as ECL indicator to develop a single-molecular immunosensor, which could detect almost 8 CEA molecules in serum sample (20 μL). CA is identified from tumor cell lines by monoclonal antibody technology, so it exhibits high accuracy in the diagnosis of specific tumors. Different types of CA, such as CA125 [88], CA153 [194,208,244] and CA 199 [245], have been chosen as the model analytes for ECL bioassays, among which CA125 is specific to epithelial ovarian cancer and endometrial cancer, CA153 is specific to breast cancer, and CA199 is specific to pancreatic cancer. Yang et al. [245] reported a disposable ECL immunosensor for CA199 detection with a special Au nanoelectrode as the platform, magnetic bead as the capture probe, and Ru(bpy)3 2+-labeled antibody as the ECL indicator, which brought hopes for the detection of biomarkers at point-of-care test. As a glycoprotein produced by yolk sac and embryonic liver, AFP has become the most sensitive biomarker of primary liver cancer. By employing two kinds of QDs with different emission colors, Zou et al. [242] proposed a spectrum-resolved ECL immunosensor which realized the simultaneous detection of CEA and AFP with improved sensitivity. Similarly, CdZnTeS QDs with both strong cathodic and anodic ECL emission behaviors were employed as ECL probes to fabricate a visual ECL sensing platform for simultaneous detections of AFP and CA125 [246]. By employing proximity hybridization strategy, Wang et al. [247] developed a facile ECL immunosensor which not only detected AFP in standard samples, but also discriminated AFP-overexpressing cells from other cell lines. PSA is a single chain glycoprotein with 237 amino acids and is known to be the most specific biomarker of prostate cancer. By using various multinary QDs as ECL indicators, Fu et al. [37,40] prepared several sandwich-type ECL immunosensors for PSA detection with wide detection range, presenting promising application potential in clinical diagnosis. As a transmembrane glycoprotein overexpressed in most tumor cells, MUC1 has been deemed as an essential tumor biomarker and a growing number of MUC1-based ECL sensors have been built by using MUC1-aptamer as the target converter [77,114,132,134,241,248]. Recently, a Ru-SiO2 NPs-based ECL aptasensor was established by using CD63 aptamer as the capturer to realize the analysis of MUC1 expression level on breast cancer cells and corresponding exosomes [248], which proposed a unique orientation for biomarker detection.

Lung cancer is one of the malignant tumors with fastest growing incidence rate and mortality rate, which holds great threat to the health and life of the population. Lung cancer biomarkers can be mainly divided into two groups, small cell lung cancer (SCLC) biomarkers such as neuron-specific enolase (NSE) and pro-gastrin-releasing peptide (Pro GRP), nonsmall cell lung cancer (NSCLC) biomarkers such as CEA, CA125, squamous cell carcinoma antigen (SCCA) and cytokeratin 19 fragment 21–1 (CY211). As for the biomarkers of SCLC, a number of ECL immunosensing methods have been proposed to detect NSE [111,205,249], while an ECL immunosensor for Pro GRP detection has also recently been reported [155]. As a kind of reliable biomarkers for NSCLC, the sensitive ECL immunosensors for SCCA has been developed [250,251]. With ZnSe QDs as the luminophores, MIL-88 MOFs as the coreaction accelerators of S2O8 2−, Deng et al. [252] fabricated a molecular imprinted polymer (MIP)-based ECL sensor to achieve the sensitive determination of SCCA down to 31 fg·mL−1. As the major and reliable biomarker of NSCLC, CY211 has become a hot detection subject, and numerous ECL immunosensors have realized the ultrasensitive quantification of CY211 with effective signal amplification strategies [201,253,254]. Different from conventional ECL detection with three-electrode system, bipolar-electrode (BPE), as a kind of electronic conductor, has drawn an ever-increasing attention in ECL bioassay owing to its advantages including ease of integration into miniaturized device and capable of visual imaging [255]. For example, Li et al. [256] constructed a BPE-based ECL immunosensor which generated coreactants in situ to trigger the luminescent of luminol, thus realizing the detection of CY211 with with excellent analysis performance.

The development of multi-immunoassay is highly-demanded to improve the accuracy for lung cancer diagnosis. To embrace this challenge, Deng et al. [257] employed Ru(bpy)3 2+ and SnS2 nanoflowers as the potential-resolved ECL labels to simultaneously detect NSE and CY211 based on ECL-RET mechanism. With PFBT Pdots as the luminophores, Ju’s group [173] proposed a true-color ECL imaging platform for simultaneous detection of CEA, NSE and CY211. The results were comparable to commercial tests, providing a reliable and simple method for lung cancer screening.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of ECL nanoemitters in detection of other disease biomarkers
ce:section.ce:para: With the acceleration of aging process among global population, heart diseases, Alzheimer’s disease and other diseases are continuously troubling the elderly. Hence, in addition to tumor biomarker detection, it is imperative to develop sensitive and specific ECL sensing platforms for the detection of other disease biomarkers. Heart diseases, such as acute myocardial infarction (AMI) and heart failure (HF), have become severe health issues, whose mortality rate is comparable to that of cancers. Cardiac troponin I (cTnI) is regarded as the golden standard for AMI screening and considerable interest has been focused on exploring highly-sensitive ECL biosensing strategies for cTnI detection [122,258]. For instance, based on a single-electrode system, Xu et al. [259] proposed a visual and high-throughput ECL immunosensor which achieved the simple but accurate determination of cTnI via employing the smartphone as the detector. Meanwhile, Liu et al. [260] employed a closed BPE to develop a tyramine-based signal amplification strategy and realize the dual-mode detection of cTnI based on the ECL signal intensity and ECL imaging changes. The emerging heart-type fatty acid-binding protein (h-FABP) is also a valuable biomarker for AMI, and has been applied as the target analyte to fabricate a self-enhanced ECL immunosensor by using Ni-TCPP MOF as the carrier of luminol [141]. As for the biomarkers of HF, compared with brain natriuretic peptide (BNP), N-terminal pro BNP (NT-proBNP) exhibits longer half-time and greater in-vitro stability. Therefore, it has attracted tremendous attentions for researchers to explore corresponding ECL bioassays [39,261]. For example, a ternary ECL system was proposed via using CeO2/SnS2 heterostructures as the coreaction accelerator to amplify the ECL response of luminol with ferritin as the carrier, which realized the accurate detection of NT-proBNP with a wide linearity range from 100 fg·mL−1 to 50 ng·mL−1 [200]. As the sensitive indicator of infection and inflammation, C-reactive protein (CRP) is also useful to evaluate patient’s risk in AMI and cardiovascular diseases [262]. Recently, the first lateral flow-based ECL immunosensor was established with Ru(bpy)3 2+ as the ECL probe, which allowed the rapid detection of CRP within 15 min due to the simple detection device and easy operation procedure [263]. Alzheimer’s disease (AD) is actually a brain disease that seriously influences the daily life of the elderly, hence, it is of particular importance to realize the early intervention of AD by developing sensitive and specific ECL immunoassays for its corresponding biomarkers detection. β-amyloid1-42 (Aβ42) oligomers with strong neurotoxicity has become an extensively-studied model analyte for the fabrication of ECL immunosensors [264–266]. To be noted, Tu et al. [267] recently proposed a dual-screening ECL bioassay by employing mesoporous silica membrane as the nano-sieving and specific aptamer as the capturer, which achieved the discrimination of Aβ monomer from Aβ oligomer and fiber, and offered a promising opportunity to detect Aβ in complex matrices.

In addition to the above protein biomarkers used for common disease diagnosis, some unusual protein biomarkers have also be involved in ECL immunoassays, such as human epididymis protein 4 (HE4) [268], β2-microglobulin [219], cysteine-rich protein 61 (CCN1) [269], cyclin D1 (CCND1) [84], phosphorylated histone H2AX (γH2AX) [270], and even the SARS-CoV-2 nucleocapsid protein [183], among which HE4 is a specific biomarker for ovarian cancer, laminin can be used for diagnosis of liver disease, lactoferrin is a representative biomarker of dry eye disease, β2-microglobulin is related to chronic kidney disease, CCN1 and CCND1 play essential roles in many cancers, and γH2AX is a biomarker for DNA damage evaluation. Notably, an ECL signal amplification strategy was proposed via employing carbon nano-horns as the photothermal convertor to elevate the electrode surface temperature, and a ratiometric ECL immunosensor was established for HE4 detection with improved sensitivity [268]. Some special biomarkers, including kidney injury molecule1 (KIM-1) [271] and collagen type Ⅳ [272], have also been employed as target analytes in ECL immunosensors for the screening of kidney injury and diabetes-related diseases, respectively. Besides the aforementioned protein biomarkers for disease diagnosis, hormone-based biomarkers and enzyme-based biomarkers have also become promising model targets for ECL bioassays. At present, the most frequently-investigated hormone-based biomarkers in ECL immunoassays are insulin [273,274], thyroid stimulating hormone (TSH) [275], procalcitonin (PCT) [276] and human chorionic gonadotrophin (HCG) [277], among which insulin is a biomarker of diabetic mellitus, TSH is related to hyperthyroidism, PCT is an indicator of inflammation, and HCG is a biomarker of pregnancy. As for enzyme-based biomarkers, thrombin [278], protein kinase A (PKA) [279], N-acetyl-β- d -glucosaminidase [213], and apurinic/apyrimidinic endonuclease 1 [209] have been widely applied as the target analytes in the construction of ECL biosensing methods.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of ECL nanoemitters in ECL imaging of protein biomarkers
ce:section.ce:para: The booming development of single cell/molecule microscopy makes the ECL imaging of cell membrane protein possible. The combination of immunoassay and ECL imaging enables the visualization of specific protein distribution on cell membrane surface, which is of great benefit to monitor the changes of target analytes in tumor and understand the complicated biological process during disease progression. In 2017, Neso Sojic and his coworkers [280] pioneered the first surface-confined ECL microscopy to image the membrane proteins of a single cell. However, it only achieved the ECL imaging of cell periphery that was in close contact with the electrode. Later, to improve the cell membrane permeability, they treated the cell with Triton-X100 and clearly observed the whole basal cell membrane with Ru(bpy)3 2+ labeled streptavidin (SA) as the ECL probe and TPrA as the coreatant [281]. Inspired by these works, substantial ECL imaging protocols have been proposed to evaluate specific protein expression level at single cell. For example, Cao et al. [282] used Ru(bpy)3 2+@SiO2/Au NPs as ECL indicators to successfully achieve the profiling of PSA expression on cell membrane via antibody-antigen immunoreaction. Liu et al. [283] used the same ECL emitters to realize the ECL imaging of single biomarkers at the electrode, and even the cytokeratin 19 distribution on MCF-7 cell. In another work, the expression changes of two membrane proteins during the cell apoptosis were simultaneously monitored using Au@L012 and g-C3N4 as two different ECL tags [284], which laid the foundation for multicolor ECL cell imaging. Nevertheless, the addition of exogenous coreactants in the above works might harm the cells. With these in mind, our group applied coreactant-embedded Pdots to label cell membrane proteins and enabled the ECL imaging of single live cell without cell permeability treatment or the addition of coreactant [182]. Similarly, a CEA-specific and guanine-rich DNA-aptamer complex was designed to recognize the overexpressed CEA on the cell membrane in which guanine was the biocompatible coreactant of Ru(bpy)3 2+, thus the ECL imaging of CEA localization at single cell was achieved without additional coreactant [285]. The above ECL imaging strategies for cytomembrane proteins mostly require cumbersome labelling process, hence, Zhang et al. [286] proposed ECL-based capacitance microscopy, which permitted the ECL visualization of 1 pg CEA at electrode and even low-content CEA at single cell without any ECL label. Though significant progress in ECL imaging has been made, the exploration of more efficient ECL nanoemitters and higher resolution ECL imaging strategy is highly demanded for better ECL single cell/molecule imaging.

The ever-growing demand for highly sensitive ECL bioassay has spurred a series of works concerning ECL signal amplification strategies. Notable progresses in the development of ECL nanoemitters and their application in sensitive detection of protein biomarkers have been witnessed in last two decades. Tables 1 and 2
summarize the representative applications of inorganic and organic ECL nanoemitters discussed below in detection of protein biomarkers, respectively.

The research field of nanoemitters-based ECL bioassay is the product of interdisciplinary integration including electrochemistry, spectroscopy, material science, bioanalytical science, and nanotechnology. For the long-term and sustainable development of nanoemitters-based ECL bioassay, the following opportunities and challenges should be considered and addressed.
(1)
ECL nanoemitters with novel excited state generation pathways. As for most of the organic ECL nanoemitters with fluorescence properties, upon electrochemical excitation, only the singlet excitons (∼25 % in total) are radiatively decayed, while the rest triplet excitons (∼75 % in total) are wasted through nonradiative decay, which causes the low ECL efficiency. To improve the utilization of triplet excitons and thus the ECL efficiency, novel organic nanoemitters with distinctive excited state generation pathways can be exploited, such as emitters with antenna effect, triplet–triplet annihilation, and thermal activation of delayed fluorescence properties.
(2)
Systematic studies on ECL mechanism. More efforts should be concentrated on investigating the relationship between the ECL behaviors of nanoemitters and the size, morphology, composition, structure of nanomaterials via employing theoretical calculation and various characterization means (such as transmission electron microscopy, electron paramagnetic resonance measurement, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy).
(3)
The specificity of ECL bioassay. The detection of target biomarkers should eliminate the interferences from the biomatrices, including pH, ionic strength and nonspecific protein adsorption. Employing efficient aptamers as the capture of target proteins is considered as a powerful alternative to improve the specificity of bioassay. Nevertheless, considering the rather limited types of aptamers used in ECL bioassay, exploring more specific aptamers for protein biomarkers detection is extremely demanded.
(4)
Advanced ECL instrument and imaging method. It is critical scientific research and commercial value to develop advanced ECL instruments such as portable ECL devices and ECL spectrometers with wide detection range. For example, compared with NIR-FL, the investigation of NIR-ECL is limited partly because the detector of ECL equipment is insensitive to the NIR luminescence. Additionally, more endeavor should be devoted to the innovation of ECL microscopy to achieve ECL imaging with highly spatiotemporal resolution and even beyond the limitation of light diffraction. The ingenious integration of ECL micro-imaging with immunoassays will afford wonderful opportunities for application of ECL imaging-based bioassays in clinical high-throughput tests.
Chao Wang: Writing – original draft. Songqin Liu: Writing – review & editing. Huangxian Ju: Writing – review & editing.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Chao Wang received her B.S. degree in Chemistry in 2013 and M.S. degree in Analytical Chemistry in 2017, both from University of Jinan. Now, she is studying in Prof. Huangxian Ju’s group at the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University, China.

Songqin Liu received his Ph.D. degrees from Nanjing University and became a full Professor of Southeast University in 2005. He was a postdoc in the group of Professor Frieder W. Scheller in University of Potsdam (Germany) from 2003 to 2004, and Lakehead University (Canada) from 2004 to 2005. His research interests focus on bioanalytical chemistry, electroanalysis and biosensing.

Huangxian Ju received his BS, MS and Ph.D. degrees from Nanjing University during 1982–1992, and became an associate and full professor of Nanjing University in 1993 and 1999. He was a postdoc in Montreal University (Canada) from 1996 to 1997, and a guest professor in the group of Professor Frieder W. Scheller in University of Potsdam (Germany) in June-July 2000. He is currently the director of State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science. His research interests focus on analytical biochemistry, biosensing and molecular diagnosis. He has authored 86 patents (42 approved), 6 English books, 7 Chinese books and 20 chapters, and published 825 papers in different journals with h-index of 101 (Google Scholar h-index 111 with more than 46000 citations).
